Burmese Tea Leaf Salad with Mutton

english: Burmese Tea Leaf Salad with Mutton
romanized: ဆိတ်လက်ဖက်ထောင်း

food_subgroup: Mixed meat salad with fermented tea leaves

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese tea leaf salad with mutton; 'salad food product' is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for lahpet thoke; A0CKP (composite meat-based salad) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to Burmese tea leaf salad with mutton; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mutton Tea Leaf Salad is approximately 200 g (one plate), providing around 860 kcal, 88 mg sodium from fish sauce, and 89 mg of cholesterol.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton TEA Leaf Salad (M13) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for M13 Mutton TEA Leaf Salad (cr_id G00010953). All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 8 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central lamb/mutton composite data (NDB 17174) proportioned to the HPB total fat figure of 44.54 g/100g, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed salad with fermented vegetables and protein
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Tea Leaf Salad. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on analogous mixed salad with fermented vegetables, protein, and high fat content. High fat (44.54 g/100g) and low carbohydrate (5.9 g/100g) substantially suppress postprandial glucose response. GL per 200 g serving = (32 × 5.9 × 2) / 100 ≈ 3.8 g, classified as low GL. Source: University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g threshold; fish sauce is the primary driver; potassium (64 mg/100g) is insufficient to offset sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Mutton Tea Leaf Salad due to its very high sodium content of 810 mg/100g; request reduced fish sauce or a low-sodium substitute, and avoid adding extra condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat content (44.54 g/100g) with zero dietary fibre increases caloric density and insulin resistance risk; saturated fat estimated at ~8 g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Mutton Tea Leaf Salad only in very small portions due to its very high fat content (44.54 g/100g) and absence of dietary fibre, which together impair insulin sensitivity; request reduced oil and fried garlic to lower fat load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 44.54 g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~8 g/100g from mutton and frying oils; cholesterol 26 mg/100g; sodium 810 mg/100g compounds cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Mutton Tea Leaf Salad due to its very high total fat (44.54 g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~8 g/100g), and sodium (810 mg/100g), all of which elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton (red meat) is rich in arachidonic acid amplifying TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; estimated saturated fat ~8 g/100g exceeds 5 g/100g threshold; chilli is a nightshade; sodium 810 mg/100g worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Mutton Tea Leaf Salad as mutton is a primary pro-inflammatory red meat, chilli is a nightshade that may trigger joint inflammation, and the high sodium content worsens RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Tea Leaf Salad (ဆိတ်လက်ဖက်ထောင်း, also known as Mutton Lahpet Thoke) is a Burmese-origin dish served in Singapore hawker and restaurant settings. It is a variant of the classic Burmese lahpet thoke (fermented tea leaf salad), distinguished by the addition of mutton (goat meat) as the primary protein. The dish is assembled by tossing fermented tea leaves (lahpet) with shredded or diced cooked mutton, roasted peanuts, toasted sesame seeds, fried garlic, dried shrimp, sliced tomato, chilli, fish sauce, and lime juice. The result is a complex, intensely flavoured salad with a combination of sour, salty, umami, and nutty notes, with a crunchy texture from the peanuts and sesame contrasting with the tender mutton and soft fermented tea leaves. Nutritionally, the dish is very high in fat (44.54 g/100g) primarily from mutton, peanuts, sesame, and fried garlic, with a moderate carbohydrate content (5.9 g/100g) and low protein (1.32 g/100g) relative to its caloric density of 430 kcal/100g. Sodium is very high at 810 mg/100g, driven by fish sauce. Dietary fibre is reported as 0 g/100g in the HPB source. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension (very high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (very high fat, zero fibre, insulin resistance risk), cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat and sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (red meat arachidonic acid, nightshade chilli). The fermented tea leaves and ginger provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols but cannot offset the pro-inflammatory profile of the red meat and high saturated fat. This dish is typically consumed as a starter or side dish at Burmese-Singaporean restaurants and hawker stalls.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010953, confidence 0.95 in source record). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb/mutton composite data as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. All other values are exact from the approved primary source.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g by HPB; this is accepted as confirmed zero per the approved HPB source, though it may reflect measurement limitations for fermented tea leaf matrix. 2. saturated_fat_g (8.0 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb/mutton composite (NDB 17174) proportioned to the HPB total fat figure; this is an approximation and should be verified if HPB publishes fatty acid breakdown. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Mutton Tea Leaf Salad preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. food_group corrected from 'Beverages / Tea and related drinks' (input error) to 'Meat and alternatives / Mixed meat salad with fermented tea leaves' — this is a solid food dish, not a beverage. 5. cultural.primary_cuisine corrected from 'Western' to 'Burmese (served in Singapore hawker context)' — the dish is of Burmese origin. 6. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on analogous mixed salad with high fat and low carbohydrate; GL per 200 g serving ≈ 3.8 g (low). 7. is_halal set to false as mutton sourcing and fish sauce halal certification cannot be confirmed without vendor-specific information.
